summary: I came to California when I was twenty-five, ready to find a job and start a new life. My first Christmas there, we were at Gaviota Beach having a picnic in bathing suits, which made me realize I liked it there. I started working in Los Angeles and eventually found my way into the entertainment industry, which shaped much of my career.

When I first came to California, I had a job as a civilian contractor on the Air Force base. It was a good starting point for me before I moved on to other opportunities in Los Angeles. That job helped me settle into the area and get accustomed to life in California.

I remember spending my first Christmas in California at Gaviota Beach, which is part of Gaviota State Park. We were in bathing suits having a picnic, which was quite unusual for Christmas but showed me how different and wonderful California was. That experience made me decide I liked living there.

My sister and brother-in-law returned to Oklahoma right after they were discharged from the Air Force. Meanwhile, I chose to stay in California and pursue my career in Los Angeles. Oklahoma was home for my family, but I was ready to make my own way out west.

After arriving in California, I went to Los Angeles to look for work and found a job at Look magazine. Los Angeles was where I really began my career in advertising and later in television. It was the hub of the entertainment industry and where I met many important people.

I worked at Look magazine for a couple of years in the Advertising Department as a secretary. It was a great introduction to the business world, and I learned a lot about advertising during that time. The home office was in New York, but I worked in the Los Angeles office, which was very active.

After Look magazine, I worked for Queen for a Day, a very successful television program that had been on radio and TV for many years. I was in the Travel Department, arranging trips for the winners, including rewards like trips to Disneyland and even Japan. The show was hosted by Jack Bailey, and I enjoyed the interesting people who came through.

Jack Bailey was the wonderful host of Queen for a Day, the show I worked on after Look magazine. He was a very nice man and part of a big, successful television program. Working on that show gave me a chance to meet many interesting people and be part of a popular series.
cues: host of Queen for a Day

summary: James Cagney's sister, Jean, was the Fashion Director of Queen for a Day, where I worked. She was a very nice lady, and it was interesting to be connected to such a famous family through the show. That connection added a special touch to my experience there.

Part of my job at Queen for a Day involved arranging travel for the winners, and one of the rewards was a trip to Japan. It was exciting to coordinate such a special prize for the queens crowned on the show. Japan was one of the more exotic destinations we handled.

I met Cleo Smith at Look magazine, and she became a good friend and important connection for me. Cleo was actually Lucy Ricardo's cousin but was raised as her sister, and she worked for Lucy at the studios. When Queen for a Day was canceled, Cleo helped me get a job working for Lucy without even an interview.
cues: Lucy Ricardo's cousin and sister figure
helped me get job with Lucy

summary: Lucy Ricardo, or Lucille Ball, was my boss for twenty-eight years after I joined her studio. I first met her during a photo shoot at her house, and she had a wonderful way of making me feel at ease. Lucy was very family oriented and had a close relationship with her cousin Cleo, who was like a sister to her.

Johnny Achison was the secretary who worked for Lucy before I joined her team. When Lucy and Desi Arnaz split up, Johnny went with Desi, leaving Lucy without a secretary. I got the job to replace him, thanks to Cleo Smith's recommendation.

Doug Kirkland was the photographer for a Look magazine interview with Lucy Ricardo that I attended before starting work for her. He was a brilliant, award-winning photographer, and it was a memorable experience to be at that photo shoot at Lucy's house. That day marked the beginning of my long association with Lucy.

When I started working for Lucy, she was in the second season of The Lucy Show, which was her second television series. Vivian Vance was still her co-star, and Gale Gordon played Mr. Mooney, the bank head managing Lucy's trust. It was a cute and popular show that continued Lucy's success after I Love Lucy.

Vivian Vance was Lucy's co-star on The Lucy Show and had been with her since I Love Lucy. She shared a house with Lucy, and they maintained a close working relationship. Vivian was part of the familiar cast that made the show enjoyable and successful.

Gale Gordon played Mr. Mooney, the head of the bank that handled Lucy's trust from her late husband, on The Lucy Show. He was still part of the cast when I started working for Lucy, adding to the show's charm and continuity. His role was important in the storyline and Lucy's on-screen life.

Desi Arnaz was Lucy's husband and business partner before they split up. After their separation, he took the secretary Johnny Achison with him, and Lucy bought him out of the studio. Desi went his own way, and Lucy became head of the studio, which was a turning point in her career and mine.

I had not seen I Love Lucy before I started working for Lucy because my family didn't have a TV in the 1950s. But I certainly knew who Lucille Ball was, as she had been in movies for twenty years before the show started. I Love Lucy was the foundation of her fame and legacy, which I became part of later.

Sam Goldwyn was the producer who gave Lucy her start as one of the Goldwyn Girls when she first came to Hollywood. Lucy was lucky to get that job, which led to a contract at RKO and helped her bring her family out from Jamestown, New York. Family was very important to Lucy from the beginning of her career.

Lucy was one of the Goldwyn Girls when she first arrived in Hollywood, thanks to Sam Goldwyn. That was her first big break in the industry and helped launch her career. It was a stepping stone before she got a contract at RKO and became a star.

Lucy brought her family from Jamestown, New York, to Hollywood when she started working in the film industry. Her mother, brother, and cousin Cleo all came out to be close to her. Family ties were very strong for Lucy, and she kept them close throughout her life.

Bob Hope was a close friend of Lucy and someone I got to meet through working with her. He was a legendary entertainer and often worked with Lucy on her shows. Meeting him was one of many memorable experiences I had in the entertainment world.

Jack Benny was another close friend and neighbor of Lucy's. He lived next door to her driveway and was known for his humor and kindness. There was a funny story Lucy told about him playing his fiddle through the kitchen while her family was having dinner, which always made everyone laugh.

Carol Burnett was one of the nicest people I met through working with Lucy. She was very kind and remained so over the years. Meeting Carol was a highlight among the many stars I encountered during my career.

Jimmy Stewart was a neighbor of Lucy's on the other side of her house on Roxbury Drive. He was known for his beautiful garden and his habit of bringing fresh vegetables to Lucy's house. Lucy loved to tell stories about him, including how he would come in the side door with a basket of vegetables and interact with her new Chinese cook.

Roxbury Drive was the street where Lucy lived, and it was famous for having many celebrities as neighbors. Jack Benny and Jimmy Stewart lived nearby, making it a unique and star-studded block in Los Angeles. Lucy often shared stories about the fun and friendly interactions among the neighbors there.

Jack Paar was a television host who aired some of Lucy's stories, including the funny ones about Jimmy Stewart and Jack Benny. Lucy enjoyed sharing her anecdotes on his show, which helped keep her lively personality in the public eye. I remember how those stories always brought laughter and warmth.

Johnny Carson was another famous TV host who featured Lucy's stories, such as the ones about her celebrity neighbors. Lucy's humor and charm were well received on his show, and it was a pleasure to see her personality shine through those appearances. Johnny Carson helped keep Lucy's legacy alive for many viewers.

We always had an office at Desilu Studios where I worked with Lucy. When the show was on hiatus, I would sometimes take work to her house to answer phones and help out. Desilu was the original home base for Lucy's productions before she sold it and moved to other studios.

After Lucy sold Desilu Studios, we moved our office to Universal Studios, where we stayed for about five years. It was another important chapter in our work life, continuing the production of Lucy's shows. Universal provided a great environment for us to keep the business running smoothly.

Following our time at Universal Studios, we moved to Warner Bros. Studios, which had formerly been the Goldwyn Studios. This was the next step in Lucy's production journey, and I continued to work closely with her there. It was part of the evolution of her career and the business side of her shows.
